topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the shared lab hummed with a grating monotony that mirrored the tightness in Elias's chest. He stared at the blueprint spread across the steel table—the intricate schematics for the 'Aetheric Resonator.' Then he looked across the room. Across the narrow aisle, Maya was tracing the exact same lines with a stylus, a faint, almost imperceptible smirk playing on her lips as she adjusted a component. A cold knot tightened in Elias’s stomach. He hadn't even bothered to check the project logs; he just *knew*. Every calculation, every theoretical leap, felt like a theft. He picked up his own set of notes, the paper feeling suddenly flimsy in his hands, and began to pace, the sound of his worn shoes echoing too loudly in the quiet space.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" Liam muttered, the word barely escaping his throat as he watched Sarah meticulously calibrate the fluid dynamics model. He leaned against the doorway, crossing his arms tightly over his chest, a rigid posture setting in. The software on his monitor displayed the identical progression curve they had been working on for the past three weeks. It wasn't just similar; it was a mirror image. A wave of heat rose in Liam’s neck, making his skin prickle. He wanted to walk over, to demand an explanation, but something held him back, a stubborn wall of forced calm. He clenched his jaw, focusing intensely on a loose wire, his knuckles whitening as he fought the urge to slam his fist onto the desk.

[story 3]

I watched Chloe from behind my coffee mug, the steam curling up to obscure my vision. Her movements were precise, almost mechanical, as she assembled the final casing for the Chronos device. It was the same design, the same obsessive attention to minute detail that I had poured into my own work. A sour taste coated my tongue. I shifted uncomfortably on the stool, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. When she finally looked up, her eyes met mine, and for a fleeting second, I saw a flicker of something—recognition? Or perhaps just the same weary resignation. I forced a neutral expression, offering a curt nod, my voice flat. "Impressive symmetry," I managed, the words sounding brittle and sharp even to my own ears.

He stared at the blueprint spread across the steel table—the intricate schematics for the 'Aetheric Resonator.' Then he looked across the room. Across the narrow aisle, Maya was tracing the exact same lines with a stylus, a faint, almost imperceptible smirk playing on her lips as she adjusted a component. A cold knot tightened in Elias’s stomach. He hadn't even bothered to check the project logs; he just *knew*. Every calculation, every theoretical leap, felt like a theft. He picked up his own set of notes, the paper feeling suddenly flimsy in his hands, and began to pace, the sound of his worn shoes echoing too loudly in the quiet space.

"Seriously?" Liam muttered, the word barely escaping his throat as he watched Sarah meticulously calibrate the fluid dynamics model. He leaned against the doorway, crossing his arms tightly over his chest, a rigid posture setting in. The software on his monitor displayed the identical progression curve they had been working on for the past three weeks. It wasn't just similar; it was a mirror image. A wave of heat rose in Liam’s neck, making his skin prickle. He wanted to walk over, to demand an explanation, but something held him back, a stubborn wall of forced calm. He clenched his jaw, focusing intensely on a loose wire, his knuckles whitening as he fought the urge to slam his fist onto the desk.

I watched Chloe from behind my coffee mug, the steam curling up to obscure my vision. Her movements were precise, almost mechanical, as she assembled the final casing for the Chronos device. It was the same design, the same obsessive attention to minute detail that I had poured into my own work. A sour taste coated my tongue. I shifted uncomfortably on the stool, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. When she finally looked up, her eyes met mine, and for a fleeting second, I saw a flicker of something—recognition? Or perhaps just the same weary resignation. I forced a neutral expression, offering a curt nod, my voice flat. "Impressive symmetry," I managed, the words sounding brittle and sharp even to my own ears.
